entry.title: SUM - DMO with SYSTEM Move : Export file transfer Speed is Very Low (RISE with SAP)
entry.content:
Hi Experts, 

Good Day.!

We have currently performing a SUM DMO with SYSTEM move and have identified that there is a Significant slowness during the data (File System Export) transfer stage (copying SUM Directory) from on Premises to Migration server (RISE Env.). 

System Information as follows: 

  • On Premises (Source) 
    • Application Server : ERP 6.0 EHP06 (NW 7.31)
    • Database : Oracle Database 12.1.0.2
    • OS : AIX 7.1
    • IBM Power Hardware.
  • Migration Server (Target)
    • Application Server : ERP 6.0 EHP07 (NW 7.40)
    • Database : HANA 2.0 SP05
    • OS : SUSE Linux Enterprise Server 15 SP06

Network Bandwidth  is 1GBps. It is dedicated line established from On-premise SAP servers to Migration server. 

The approach is SUM DMO with SYSTEM move, after executing the SUM on source system, we have copied the SUM directory from source to target (SUM directory size is approximately 400GB).

But the copying speed of SUM directory is less than 4mbps. 

The estimated time is 2days. So we could not perform migration in Production (Live system) due long downtime period. We have used scp command to move the files between servers. 

I would like to know how that;

  1. What could be the possible reason for such kind of low transfer throughput despite having a dedicated 1 Gbps link?
  2. Are there any recommended tools, parameters (OS, SAP) or best practices from SAP for transferring the SUM directory?
  3. Has anyone experience similar kind of issue?

Thank you in advance for your support.

Best Regards,

Nishatharu

entry.title: Re: Claude Code via MCP: Poor man's Joule, or a practical tool?
entry.content:
So...

Another short session tonight. Yesterday I had Claude Code generate a RAP Application based on the Northwind Traders data model. He did it 98% correctly, with only one minor error that I corrected manually.

Today I asked him to generate a local Fiori app for it. To make the prompt better, I used the prompt improver at https://platform.claude.com. If you don't know it, it's a tool that you give it a crappy, simplistic prompt, and it improves it. It's not free, but costs a few cents per prompt. OpenAI also has a tool like that in their Console.

Anyways, this was the prompt it came up with:

You will be creating a local Fiori application based on an existing RAP (RESTful ABAP Programming) application. Your task is to generate the necessary code and configuration to build a Fiori app that allows users to maintain and display lists of various business objects.

Here is the package containing the RAP application you should use as the foundation: ZNORTH.

The local file system path where the Fiori app should be created is: /Users/kamilkovac/Code/Working/North.

The app should provide maintenance and list display capabilities for the following objects:

  • Categories
  • Customers
  • Products
  • Suppliers
  • Orders with items

Your task is to:

  1. Analyze the RAP application structure in the specified package to understand the data model and services
  2. Create a local Fiori application at the specified path
  3. Implement maintenance (create, read, update, delete) functionality for each of the listed objects
  4. Implement list display views for each of the listed objects
  5. Ensure proper navigation between list views and maintenance screens
  6. Configure the app to connect to the RAP backend services

Before providing your implementation, use the scratchpad to:

  • Identify the key RAP entities and services in the package
  • Plan the Fiori app structure (manifest.json, views, controllers)
  • Determine the UI5 controls needed for each object type
  • Map out the navigation flow between different views

Think through your implementation approach here. Consider: - What RAP entities exist in the package? - What Fiori app template is most appropriate (List Report, Freestyle, etc.)? - How will you structure the views and controllers? - What routing configuration is needed? - How will CRUD operations be implemented for each object?

After your planning, provide your implementation. Your response should include:

  1. Project Structure: Outline the folder and file structure for the Fiori app
  2. Configuration Files: Provide the content for manifest.json, package.json, and any other necessary configuration files
  3. View Definitions: Provide the XML view definitions for list and maintenance screens for each object
  4. Controller Logic: Provide the JavaScript controller code for handling user interactions and CRUD operations
  5. Navigation Configuration: Explain how navigation between views is configured
  6. Service Integration: Show how the app connects to the RAP backend services

Format your response with clear section headers and code blocks. Use appropriate XML tags to organize different parts of your implementation.

Your final output should be a complete, working Fiori application structure that can be deployed to the specified local path. Include all necessary files with their full content, not just snippets. Make sure to provide specific implementation details rather than general descriptions.

 

Yeah, it's a mouthful. Cost me 8 cents of a Euro. Money well spent.

With this, Claude created a Freestyle Fiori app in 12 minutes. Sorry, forgot to take a screenshot before he compacted the conversation (Claude Code compacts the conversation when the context window fills up).

Did the app work? No. It wasn't even showing any data. Poor Antigravity Agent had to spend another half an hour fixing it, because I hit the limit on Claude session tokens (with Sonnet, but on High effort):

Of course, after each one of these 6 steps the Agent came back declaring that it was done. I had to ask him to go back and check again. Now I understand why someone invented the Ralph Wiggum Loop.

Then finally the app started working:

    

Next I asked the Antigravity Agent (Gemini 3.1 Pro High) for a few small changes, which went smoothly. Things like add navigation from Product Categories to Products (in addition to Category Detail), display all Prices with 2 decimals - stuff like that.

Next I switched to Codex. First time I've used Codex CLI - been using the Desktop app so far. It offered me to use the new GPT 5.4 - why not.

I must say I prefer the CLI tools. The only thing I'm missing is that you can't paste screenshots.

I asked Codex to add CRUD Operations also for Categories, Products, Suppliers and Customers (only Orders and Items had them). He did it quite well in the backend, created all the necessary artefacts with zero errors:

Encouraged by this success, I asked him to also adjust the local Fiori app to reflect these changes.

And he broke it. It is no longer possible to create / update Order items with price information.

What is more surprising is that none of the three Models is able to fix it. Not Codex, not Claude, not Gemini. This is shocking to me. I thought they were especially good at this part. I would understand if they didn't know ABAP - they didn't have much chance to learn. But front-end?!

What gives? Am I missing something?

I must be missing something. 😤

 

One thing was interesting to observe: Gemini in Antigravity has access to Browser Control. While debugging, it would launch a Chrome window in a sort of a sandbox (the window borders were glowing blue to indicate this). The Agent would take screenshots, read the DOM and the Console log, then manipulate the cursor - click on stuff, enter stuff into screen fields:

I have always thought Computer Use was a dead end. Forcing agents to use web pages meant for humans is crap and wastes a boatload of tokens. It's like when we were transitioning from analogue CRT computer screens to digital LCD ones: The computers at that time had only analogue VGA ports. So the computer would translate its digital output into analogue VGA only to transmit it through a cable to an LCD monitor, which would convert it from analogue back to digital.

It's the same thing all over again. We are translating digitally-stored information into analogue form (text and buttons on web pages), then forcing digital entities to look at it, decipher it, and convert back to their native language.

Fortunately, it won't be like this for long: A new Protocol called WebMCP is in the works. Read all about it. 

(Another initiative in this direction is AI Workers by Cloudfare, which can "wrap a legacy site in an API‑like layer, for example, returning JSON chunks of content, simplified DOM, or specialised endpoints that agents can call, rather than raw HTML".)

 

In any case, it was interesting to observe Browser Control for the first time, and I admit that for cases like these it has its raison d'être, temporary as it may be.

 

Next: Maybe some architecting? Another take at a web app? More data analytics, perhaps by means of ad-hoc CDS Views? This thing can write a CDS View before you can say Structured Query Language.

Or an n8n automation that calls an SAP endpoint? I am running an n8n Community Edition on a VPS, not using it much...

We'll see.

entry.title: Re: Reverse Payment Run
entry.content:
Dear @raphaelbucheli,

This is expected behavior in Public Cloud. SAP states that Reverse Payment Run cannot be used once payment media have already been created, once entries exist in the payment/check register, or once the payment is already in approval. SAP also states that a payment medium is created with each successful payment run. Therefore, after a normal successful payment run, this job template has only a very limited usage window in practice

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/039683576ea1a96be10000000a4450e5.html

If payment media already exist the supported SAP approach is to use the cleanup/reset process in Manage Payment Media and Manage Automatic Payments, rather than Reverse Payment Run

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3285159

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3374400

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/031c345485b84c8c94265be9ef61d3a8/0bf4918dd83a4e92bc87fc575166e93f.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Sum field in ALV not updating after manual row insertion
entry.content: If you use CL_GUI_ALV_GRID, by default, all ALV Grid control events don't trigger the PAI (system events). Specifically, if there are fields around the ALV Grid in the Dynpro screen, the specific buttons added to the integrated toolbar will not trigger the PAI and therefore will not transmit the values. This can be changed for all events via the parameter I_APPL_EVENTS = 'X' of the constructor (application events). Note that you have to call CL_GUI_CFW=>DISPATCH during the PAI to trigger the logic behind the control events (I guess you should do it at the beginning of the PAI).

entry.title: How to debug inside async update task via Eclipse ADT?
entry.content:
I can't find an option in Eclipse ADT to debug a V1 update function module (V1 = start immediately) called "in update task".

How to do it?

Thanks.

Sandra

My system: Eclipse 2025-09, ADT 3.52.3, ABAP 7.58 SP 1.

entry.title: Re: How to debug inside async update task via Eclipse ADT?
entry.content:
The option is currently only available via SAP GUI for Windows (not available in SAP GUI inside Eclipse ADT):

  1. Type /H and Enter to start the debugger, change the debugger setting "Update task". You may either save it as a default setting, or set it only once.
  2. Run your code.
  3. When the first update function module starts, the debugger stops at the first line and the function module source code is shown in ADT if ADT was already running, otherwise it shows up in SAP GUI for Windows.

Notes:

  1. I don't know what happens if the update function module belongs to a function group flagged as system program (you may need to also activate the system debugger).
  2. I don't know what happens if the update task is started as a local update task (ABAP statement "SET UPDATE TASK LOCAL"), maybe you can fully debug via ADT.

entry.title: Re: Urgent: Process to Delete Uploaded Bank Statement
entry.content:
Dear @PradipSarkar,

The uploaded bank statement is handled in the Manage Bank Statements app. If the statement is still in Received and Saved status, you can delete it there directly SAP also confirms that this app supports both Delete bank statements and Reverse bank statements

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/918bca53037f408f91a2295d04ac16bc/e74a1f567afbdf21e10000000a4450e5.html

If the Delete button is greyed out SAP states that in production a bank statement can only be deleted when it is in Received and Saved status. For other statuses such as Not Received or Partially Applied the supported SAP action is to use Reverse in Manage Bank Statements this reverses the related journal entries and removes the bank statement entries from the backend tables

Steps to do:

  1. Open Manage Bank Statements
  2. Search for the statement
  3. Check the status
  4. If status = Received and Saved choose Delete
  5. If Delete is not available choose Reverse instead

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3616575

IMPORTANT: If the bank statement was already cancelled/reversed and you still see the cash flow in One Exposure / Cash Flow Analyzer, SAP says you must remove those flows separately in Delete Data from One Exposure with source application BS

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3091261

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Group Reporting Manual JE Not Picked in 3A Investment Elimination
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are attempting to post a manual Group Reporting journal entry to adjust the USD value for the following:

  • Consolidation Unit: DE01
  • FS Item: 172***
  • G/L Account: 180*****
  • Partner Unit: DE02

Our expectation is that the 3A – Investment Elimination job should consider this manual adjustment when performing the elimination. However, during testing, we observed that the job is only eliminating the 0F balance, and the manual adjustment posted via the Group Reporting JE is being ignored.

For testing purposes, I recorded this entry in the Q system (see attached example).

Questions:

  1. Does the upload template (attached) need to be modified for this adjustment to be considered during the 3A elimination?
  2. Should a different document type or posting category be used for manual Group Reporting JEs to be picked up by the Investment Elimination?
  3. Are there any additional flags or attributes required on the Group Journal Entry to ensure it is included in the elimination logic?

Thanks in advance for your guidance.

Best Regards

Naveen Kumar

entry.title: Major New Version of the hana-cli: How It Was Built with the Help of AI (and a Lot of Human Review)
entry.content:
This week I released the newest hana-cli version, and I wanted to share what actually happened behind the scenes. Not just the feature list, but the real process since this was the first version with significant AI assistance. The small steps, the rough edges, and the lessons learned.

TL;DR Code generation helped the project add more features in two months than I'd been able to deliver in the last two years; but quality still came from discipline, architecture, and a ton of testing.

hana-cli HanaCloud command that lists details of HANA Cloud Instances for the target BTP SubAccount

What Changed in the 4.0 Release

4.0 release was not a small maintenance version. It was a structural and architectural shift for the tool.

In this release, I pushed major updates across runtime, architecture, test coverage, docs, and developer workflow. Looking at the git history since January 2026, the repo saw 235 commits so far, with a huge concentration in February and March. This was not a patch release cycle. This was the kind of work that changes how a tool feels to use (hopefully for the better).

The version line moved to 4.x, which tells you something important: substantial internal changes happened. We migrated to Express 5, now that CAP supports it. The MCP server evolved from an experimental rough draft into something that actually feels almost production-ready, with stronger tool registration patterns, better resource handling, and practical guidance for setup and troubleshooting.

Under the hood, database connection handling had to be redesigned to support MCP and multi-environment execution better. CLI startup performance improved significantly through lazy loading and faster execution paths. This is an item that's bothered me personally for a while and that I've tried to tackle in the past. We made import and export commands much stronger, adding richer import options, better schema handling, and more thoughtful validation behavior but still with a strict focus on keeping the processing client side. Internationalization was finally a focus (thanks to machine translation), with full translation bundles for additional locales like German, Spanish, French, Japanese, Korean, Portuguese, Simplified Chinese, Hindi, and Polish.

One of the bigger quality-of-life improvements was consistency work across our very large command surface. This is where AI coding assistants really shine. They helped normalize parameter naming, improved how the tool suggests fixes for unknown commands and options, and made sure 200+ commands follow the same conventions. The docs got rebuilt from scratch with a modern VitePress site and better technical guides. And the tests scaled hard. We went from roughly 40 percent coverage on critical paths to roughly 85 percent. That matters more than people think. And this was actually the first thing I did before I would start using AI. Without more tests I wouldn't have trusted AI to make some of the consistency changes. In the end, the unit and E2E tests will really help when introducing new features in the future, because after each small change you can run the entire battery of tests. If it's just me testing by hand I often can't test every edge case or every command variation. With a strong test suite, I can be more confident that the changes I make do not break existing behavior.

The Architecture Shift That Matters Most

For me, the most important architecture story in 4.x is this: we moved from "a big bag of commands" toward "a more intentional platform". Instead of four separate operating modes living in isolation, CLI mode, interactive mode, API server mode, and MCP mode now feel like parts of one coherent system. That might sound abstract, but it changed how I approach new features.

The MCP server is a good example. It went from experimental command passthrough to something that actually feels like a real integration layer. Instead of duplicating command definition, it dynamically reads from the CLI setup or from the help output. We built better registration patterns, better metadata handling, and practical troubleshooting guidance. When I want to add a new command now, I do not have to think about five different places to integrate it. The pattern is clear: dynamic discovery and reuse was the only way to continue to scale.

Connection and execution flows became more deliberate too. We handle profile-based and CDS-based cases much more consistently now, with stronger error messaging and fewer of those fragile edge cases that only show up in production. Performance was treated as a product feature, not an afterthought. Startup path optimization and lazy loading were not "nice to have". They changed day-to-day usability in a real way for common, frequent used commands.

hana-cli UI mode with SAPUI5 based interface

How I Used GitHub Copilot in Small, Incremental Steps

I did not sit down and ask for one giant AI-generated rewrite. That sounded like a recipe for disaster to me.

Instead, I worked in small, intentional slices. Start with one behavior. Write a few targeted tests. Implement a narrow change. Re-run all the tests. Refine. Repeat. This rhythm sounds obvious until you are staring at the opportunity to ask Copilot to "just generate the whole feature", which is precisely when discipline matters most.

A key part of the workflow was starting with hand-coded examples first. I wrote the initial 7 to 8 unit tests manually to establish the baseline style and what really mattered. Those tests acted like calibration points. They showed the agent what I thought good tests look like in this codebase. After that, I used the coding agent to extrapolate safely. Generate related scenarios. Extend edge-case coverage. Replicate patterns across similar commands. Fill out the repetitive but necessary test variations. That gave me speed without surrendering control.

The other way that I used AI was for parallel work. This project has long been a weekend/late night/passion project for me. This isn't part of my day job. But free time is precious and limited. I've had a long list of features and wishes for this project that just never seemed to get shorter. With AI coding agents I could kick off tasks while I'm working during the day. I could go to a meeting for TechEd 2026 and in my other window, an AI agent would be running tests or renaming parameters. At the end of the day I could review the results, make adjustments, and keep the momentum going. It turned AI from a "weekend experiment" into a "day-to-day teammate". Although a teammate that sometimes felt like it had amnesia. Occasionally it would repeat a mistake it made a few days prior. Or it would completely forget a critical architectural pattern that I had painstakingly documented in the repo instructions. That is where the next section comes in.

Why the .github Folder Was Critical

If I had to point to one thing that made AI-assisted work sustainable in this repo, it is the .github customization layer. This is where the project-specific memory and operating model live. No longer did have a team of minions with short term memory loss, but a team of agents with a shared playbook and role-specific instructions. I finally started seeing real consistency across multiple requests.

Project Structure and the .github folder with content that influences GitHub CoPilot

Here is how each layer of that system actually works.

copilot-instructions.md — the always-on baseline

This file is loaded into every single agent session when this project is the target, regardless of what you ask or which files are open. It is the unconditional context layer. In this project it does a few things: it redirects the agent to the project-overview.instructions.md file for deeper architecture context, it states the non-negotiables (Node.js ESM module format, i18n text bundles for every user-facing string, VitePress docs structure), and it sets the behavioral guardrails (minimal changes, no new dependencies without human approval, do not touch docs or workflows unless explicitly asked). Think of it as the onboarding document that every new teammate has to read before their first commit. The agent reads it before every conversation.

.github/instructions/*.instructions.md — file-scope rules with automatic injection

This repo has 25 instruction files, each targeted at a specific area: cli-command-development.instructions.md, testing.instructions.md, route-development.instructions.md, i18n-translation-management.instructions.md, mcp-server-development.instructions.md, vitepress-config-management.instructions.md, and so on. Each file has an applyTo front-matter field that is a glob pattern, for example bin/*.js or tests/**/*.Test.js or docs/.vitepress/**/*.{ts,js,json}. When the agent touches a file that matches a glob, the corresponding instruction file is automatically pulled into context without you having to ask. So when the agent is editing a command file in bin/, it automatically picks up the rules about yargs structure, required JSDoc, i18n bundle usage, error handling shape, and database connection patterns. When it is editing a test file, it automatically picks up the Mocha conventions, assertion style, and the utilities it should reuse. This is the mechanism that prevented AI "drift". The rules followed the files, not the conversation.

.github/agents/*.agent.md — specialized agent personas

Each agent file defines a named role that can be invoked explicitly. The YAML declares a name, a description, and a list of allowed tools. The body defines the agent's job, its constraints, its approach steps, and its expected output format. The CLI Agent (cli.agent.md) is scoped to bin/, routes/, utils/, and related tests and is explicitly constrained from touching documentation unless asked. The DocOps Agent (docops.agent.md) knows the docs taxonomy and sidebar structure. The MCP Agent (mcp.agent.md) enforces JSON-RPC compliance and tool registration patterns. The Tooling Agent (tooling.agent.md) handles automation scripts, npm scripts, and GitHub workflows with an eye on cross-platform compatibility. The Version Maintenance Agent orchestrates the full release choreography: version bumps across multiple package.json files, SAPUI5 version updates, changelog management, and documentation synchronization. The E2E Test Agent specializes in multi-step CLI testing scenarios. What this means in practice is that the agent does not have to figure out what role to play from scratch each time. It is handed a specific operating mode with explicit scope and constraints baked in.

.github/skills/*/SKILL.md — bundled, reusable workflows

Skills are a level above agents. Where an agent defines a persona, a skill defines a procedure. Each skill folder contains a SKILL.md that lists the steps to follow for a complete workflow, the instruction files that apply, and the references the agent should load. This repo has three skills: cli-command-development, docs-automation, and mcp-server-workflows. The cli-command-development skill, for example, tells the agent to load the CLI command instructions, the route instructions if a route file is involved, the i18n instructions, the testing instructions, and the test-utilities-reuse instructions, then work through them in sequence. Skills eliminate the overhead of re-explaining which instruction files are relevant and in what order to apply them. They encode accumulated workflow knowledge so the agent does not have to reconstruct it on the fly.

.github/prompts/*.prompt.md — ready-made entry points for common tasks

Prompt files are parameterized task templates. Each one has a name, a description, an argument-hint that tells the user what input to provide, and a body that pre-fills the task framing the agent would otherwise have to reconstruct. For example, update-markdown-docs.prompt.md instructs the agent to read the existing doc style, follow the command-documentation rules when working in docs/02-commands/, keep changes minimal, and then report both the edits and a consistency cross-check. Other prompts cover updating GitHub workflows, VitePress config, MCP server code, tsconfig files, and package.json scripts. Using a prompt file instead of a freeform question means the agent starts from a well-defined task structure rather than having to interpret a vague request and risk misunderstanding the scope.

.github/hooks/ and quality-gates.json — deterministic guardrails

This is the most technical layer. The hooks are three Node.js scripts — pre-tool-use.js, post-tool-use.js, and stop.js — wired up via quality-gates.json as lifecycle interceptors. The quality-gates.json file maps hook names to commands: PreToolUse runs pre-tool-use.js before every tool call, PostToolUse runs post-tool-use.js after every tool call that modifies files, and Stop runs stop.js when the agent finishes a session. The pre-hook inspects the tool call payload and blocks a list of risky commands outright: npm install, npm publish, git push, git reset --hard, rm -rf, and similar destructive or irreversible operations.

If the agent tries to run any of those, the hook intercepts it and returns a blocking response before the command ever executes. The post-hook checks what files changed in the working tree after each tool use, runs targeted lint checks if source files changed, and surfaces any new errors to the agent so it can self-correct before moving to the next step. The stop hook runs a final summary of what changed in the session. This made a real difference: instead of discovering that the agent had introduced a linting error three steps after the fact, the post-hook caught it immediately and the agent fixed it in place. It turned error correction from a cleanup pass at the end into a continuous loop within the session.

This structure sounds elaborate until you use it. What it does is turn Copilot from "autocomplete with opinions" into "a teammate with repo-specific understanding".

The agent stayed aligned with project patterns, especially around ESM syntax, i18n text handling, and command conventions. I could steer style and scope per task type instead of hoping the AI would guess correctly. Generated code drifted away less from project norms. I got safer behavior around risky commands through hooks and guardrails. After you read a few horror stories online about AI wiping out a repo or an entire drive, you see why this is so important. And we preserved architectural context across long sessions and many edits so the tool never forgot why things were designed a certain way.

Each agent came with matching instruction files that spelled out the specific rules, patterns, and anti-patterns for that domain. When I needed to add a new command, I asked the CLI Agent, and it immediately understood command structure, i18n requirements, test patterns, and where to hook into the command metadata. When I wanted to regenerate all command docs at once, I asked the DocOps Agent, and it knew the taxonomy categories, sidebar structure, and the exact generation scripts to use.

hana-cli's New Documentation Site

Fast Code Generation Is Real, but So Is Review Work

Code generation is fast. Review is not optional.

Even with strong prompts and repo instructions, I still spent many full days validating behavior changes across a 200-plus command surface. Checking cross-command consistency, particularly parameter naming, schema alignment, and error handling patterns. Running tests and fixing failures, especially the tricky cross-platform compatibility issues between Windows, Linux, and macOS. Reviewing docs and examples for completeness and accuracy. Manually verifying edge cases that looked correct but were not robust enough. Testing UI components and WebSocket behavior in the browser. Validating MCP tool registrations and JSON-RPC message flow.

This is the part some people skip when they tell the AI productivity story as pure Vibe coding. The output quality that we hopefully have in hana-cli came from that review loop, not from generation alone.

The Testing Story

One of the biggest wins this cycle was test coverage. We went from something like 40 percent coverage on critical paths to around 85 percent. Did code generation write all those tests? No. But it made it fast enough that writing comprehensive tests became practical instead of aspirational.

The pattern that worked was to hand-write 7 to 8 seed tests that show the ideal test structure and what you actually care about validating. Then ask the agent to extend those, creating related scenarios, error cases, and variations. Verify every generated test by running it locally and checking it actually catches bugs. Keep the tests you trust, delete the rest. By the end, we had better coverage and faster iteration. Both mattered.

What Took the Most Time

If you ask me where the real work happened, it was not generation. Creating the code went from taking most of the time to probably only about 5% of the time. Architecture decisions about how commands should register with the MCP server. Parameter passing design. Connection flow refactoring. It was writing the tests first, before or alongside the feature. It was validating that the generated code actually works by running it, breaking it on purpose, fixing it. It was keeping docs in sync every time we changed command behavior. It was cross-platform testing to ensure Windows and Linux do not have surprises. And it was reviewing for consistency, making sure 200-plus commands all follow the same conventions.

That is all human work. Generation helped with the repetitive parts. Discipline came from the human part of the project.

My Honest Take After This Release

Copilot did not replace engineering here. It amplified it.

The wins came from combining clear architecture that you can reference and extend. Repo-specific agent customization so the tool understands your project, not just general code. Small incremental development instead of big risky changes. Human-authored seed tests so the AI has good examples to learn from. And relentless validation through testing and review of every piece.

If you are building serious tooling, that balance works. Skip the validation half and you are gambling.

For this release, I am proud of both the speed and the discipline. And yes, I am also proud that we made it feel better for users while modernizing a lot under the hood.

Special thanks to everyone who uses hana-cli and reports issues. That feedback drives where we invest next.

entry.title: Unveiling a Fresh Look of SAP Learning’s Artificial Intelligence Page + Beyond Hype Live Sessions
entry.content:
Have you checked out the revamped Artificial Intelligence page on SAP Learning? If not, now is the perfect time to explore its sleek new design and discover a wealth of resources to empower your AI journey. Whether you're curious about the basics of Artificial Intelligence or eager to dive into SAP Business AI solutions, this updated page is your gateway to actionable knowledge.

But that’s not all—SAP Learning is taking education beyond the hype with "Beyond Hype: The AI Series", a series of live, fast-paced 45-minute sessions designed to help you not just talk about AI but use it with confidence. These sessions, led by inspiring Joule and AI Foundation experts, offer tangible, applied learning to help you adopt and apply SAP Business AI solutions effectively.

Why Join Beyond Hype?

  • Practical Learning: These aren’t just theoretical talks; these sessions are designed to help you turn AI concepts into real-world applications.
  • Expert Guidance: Gain insights from top AI minds who understand what it takes to leverage AI within SAP solutions.
  • Convenient Format: Short, engaging sessions that fit into your busy schedule.
  • On-Demand Access: Missed a session? Don’t worry—you can catch up with recordings anytime.

The revamped page highlights these sessions with an eye-catching banner that links directly to the Beyond Hype Session Catalogue, your one-stop destination for registering for upcoming live sessions and accessing previously recorded ones. It’s all about seamless navigation and empowering learning.

Head over to Artificial Intelligence at SAP Learning to experience the new look and feel and take your first step toward mastering AI. Start using it today with Beyond Hype!

entry.title: Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Adriano Marques
entry.content:
Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Adriano Marques

Esta publicação faz parte da série Somos SAP Community Curitiba.

A ideia da série foi apresentada neste post de abertura:
Introdução - Série da comunidade: Somos SAP Community Curitiba

Fui convidado por @SimoneAzevedo para participar da série e compartilhar um pouco da minha jornada no ecossistema SAP.
Você pode conferir o post dela aqui:
Somos SAP Community Curitiba – Simone Azevedo - SAP Community

👨‍💻Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?

Sou Adriano Marques, empresário e curador da SAP Community de Curitiba.  Meu papel é contribuir  no fortalecimento e conexão dos profissionais SAP na região.

💙O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?

O que mais me motiva são as pessoas e a troca genuína de conhecimento.  Projetos SAP costumam ser desafiadores, complexos e cheios de aprendizado. A comunidade cria um espaço onde profissionais podem compartilhar experiências reais — sucessos, desafios e lições aprendidas. 

Cada encontro da comunidade mostra algo muito claro:  quando profissionais compartilham conhecimento, todos evoluem.

🚀Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

Na conexão com profissionais com experiências e especialidades diferentes — consultores, arquitetos, gestores e especialistas — criou um ambiente extremamente rico de aprendizado para mim. O networking agregou na minha jornada.

Mas talvez o maior impacto seja outro: A comunidade cria oportunidades verdadeiras entre pessoas que acreditam em colaboração, aprendizado contínuo e evolução profissional.

❓Que conselho você daria para quem está começando agora no ecossistema SAP e ainda não conhece a comunidade?

Venha participar da nossa comunidade. Não perca tempo. Seu networking irá crescer imensamente, principalmente no início de sua carreira. Boas coisas acontecem quando estamos juntos.  

🌎Conecte-se comigo

LinkedIn 👉 https://www.linkedin.com/in/adrianomoliveira

✨Convidados para continuar a série

Para continuar a série Somos SAP Community Curitiba, convido:

 @Natacha_Wistuba @david_vilela88 @MarcosFerreira @davidson_alves_ti @Tiago_Fakeiti1 @beatrizsakai 

a compartilharem suas histórias na comunidade.

Além das perguntas da série:

1️⃣Quem sou eu na SAP Community Curitiba?
2️⃣O que mais me motiva a continuar fazendo parte da SAP Community Curitiba?
3️⃣Como fazer parte da comunidade impactou minha jornada profissional ou pessoal?

E deixo também uma pergunta adicional:

❓Para você, quais são as vantagens de participar da comunidade SAP ? Cite pelos menos 3.

✨Frase final

Tecnologia conecta sistemas.Comunidades conectam pessoas.  Cada história compartilhada fortalece ainda mais essa comunidade.

#SomosSAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunityCWB
#SAPCommunity

the service supports flexible repository usage. Organizations can:

For the last 15 years specialized DMS vendors that integrate to ECC and now to S4HANA using ECM or archive link technology are the to-go DMS tools for customers to store attachments and access them in S4HANA Fiori apps via workspaces tab…. And those vendors are promoting AI capabilities to explore the content there, even pulled from S4HANA fiori app (ea. Maintenance request/Notification) But there are some customers that use SAP S4HANA DMS still  (with very limited capabilities in comparison), which looks this BTP tools improves.
 
How would this SAP BTP Document Management work in this scenario?   What is the licensing schema?

entry.title: Key Skills Every SAP Consultant Needs in 2026
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP consultants can no longer focus on one tool. Projects now demand broader technical ownership. Clients expect speed, clarity, and stable systems. This requires strong core skills and smart choices.

1. Strong S/4HANA Technical Foundation

Deep S/4HANA knowledge is mandatory. This includes data model changes and CDS views. Understand embedded analytics and Fiori architecture. Know where custom code still makes sense.

2. Clean Core Understanding

Clean core is no longer optional. Custom code must be reduced and controlled. Know what belongs in S/4HANA. Know what belongs outside the core. Follow SAP guidance, not shortcuts. Shortcuts always create future issues.

3. SAP BTP Hands-on Experience

SAP BTP skills are critical in 2026. Extensions move away from the core system. Know CAP, RAP, and side-by-side patterns. Understand event-driven and API-based designs. You must build, not just explain.

4. Integration Knowledge

Most SAP systems do not work alone. Integration skills are now core skills. Understand SAP Integration Suite basics. Know IDocs, APIs, and event messaging. Debugging integration issues is a daily task.

5. ABAP Skills That Still Matter

ABAP is not dead. Bad ABAP is. Know modern ABAP syntax. Avoid outdated patterns and heavy reports. Write clean, readable, testable code.

6. Fiori and UX Awareness

Users judge systems by screens. Fiori is no longer optional. Understand standard apps before custom ones. Avoid rebuilding what SAP already provides. Simple UX reduces training and errors.

7. Security and Authorizations

Security issues stop projects instantly. Technical consultants must understand roles. Know basic authorization objects. Understand transport and access risks. Never treat security as an afterthought.

8. Automation and Tools

Manual work does not scale. Automation saves time and reduces errors. Use ATC, code inspector, and test tools. Learn basic scripting where useful. Tools make good consultants faster.

9. Communication with Non-Technical Teams

Technical skill alone is not enough. You must explain problems clearly. Use simple words with business users. Avoid technical terms unless required. Clear communication builds trust quickly.

10. Learning Discipline

SAP changes every year. Skills expire faster than before. Read SAP notes and blogs often. Practice in real systems, not slides.

Learning must be continuous and active.

Conclusion

SAP consultants in 2026 must be builders. They must also be careful decision-makers. Strong basics, clean design, and clear thinking matter most.

Trends change. Core skills stay.

entry.title: Re: Can the system notify managers when a team member rec...
entry.content:
DearJaime, 

If the requirement is to trigger a notification specifically based on the rating once the form is completed, the ratings could be pushed to a custom MDF object via Integration Center and an email notification triggered. 

However, in this specific case, you could instead support managers in working proactively. This can be achieved by using Calibration alerts and notifications to surface this information earlier and/or by triggering a Business Rule to add review steps that account for scenarios where the manually entered overall form rating falls below a defined threshold.  

Finally, a performance review status report highlighting such cases could also be provided to managers, enabling them to anticipate and prepare for upcoming process steps. Finally, it may also be worth staying tuned for future product updates in 2026.

Hope this helps.
Thank you,
Nicolas

entry.title: #CoE#TFAL Treatment of Opening Balances After Historical Balance Migration
entry.content:
(S/4HANA Public Cloud – Fiscal Year April to March)

To align expectations on how opening balances are handled in S/4HANA Public Cloud, please see the explanation below based on our migration and posting approach.

Fiscal Year Definition

  • Fiscal Year runs April to March
    • Example: FY2025 = April 2024 to March 2025

FY2025 – Historical Balance Migration

  • Periods 1 to 12 of FY2025 were migrated using the Historical Balance migration object.
  • The Historical Balance object posts period‑specific balances but automatically reverses the cumulative balance at year‑end.
  • As a result:
    • The cumulative balance at the end of FY2025 is zero
    • No balance is carried forward to the next fiscal year

Impact:

  • The opening balance of FY2026 is zero, which is expected system behavior when Historical Balance is used for a full fiscal year.

FY2026 – Transition Year Approach

  • Periods 1 to 5 (FY2026):
    • Migrated using Historical Balance in the Migration Cockpit
  • Period 6 (FY2026):
    • Migrated using GL Balance and Open Items objects to establish a proper carry‑forward base
  • Periods 7 to 12 (FY2026):
    • Posted through normal business transactions

Impact:

  • From this point onward, balances are system‑driven and continuous.

Year‑End Carryforward (FY2026 → FY2027)

  • At the end of FY2026:
    • Balance Sheet accounts will be carried forward automatically to FY2027 opening balances
    • P&L accounts will be closed and carried forward to Retained Earnings
  • This restores standard SAP year‑end behavior from FY2026 onwards.

Is this a best‑practice approach for S/4HANA Public Cloud implementations?

entry.title: Re: Import Performance Objectives - How to add the associ...
entry.content:
Dear Sofia Anna, 

To update existing goals with Metric LookUp (MLT) data in SAP SuccessFactors Goal Management using Beta Goal Import, please follow these steps:

1. Find existing goal's internal ID using report (eg. Ad Hoc Goal Management Report)

2. Download the import Goal plan template CSV header (as you did to import goals initially)

3. Create your CSV file with the necessary fields considering the internal goal ID in the proper column. Kindly refer to these detailed guidelines to ensure also proper ACTION column (UPDATE) 2072059 - Beta Goal Import Guide for SuccessFactors Goal Management - SAP for Me . This article also include a fantastic file sample : EXAMPLE_KPI_Import_Goals_Create_File_Change_File.csv as attachment.

4. Upload the import file.

5. MLT table rows are updated using their SUBGUID.

6. Control your upload via reporting or proxy depending on your permissions.

Tip(s): Ensure the goal plan template is in the latest version to avoid import errors after upgrading.

Please let me know if this solution works for you.

Hope this helps.

Thank you

Nicolas

entry.title: Re: I want to extract performance review form content fro...
entry.content: Dear Anuja, in SAP SuccessFactors, Performance Review form content can be extracted either via API or Reporting. For automated extraction or integrations, the SAP SuccessFactors OData API can be used to retrieve form data (e.g., form headers, sections, and ratings). For reporting or manual exports, the recommended approach is to use SAP SuccessFactors People Analytics (Story/Table reports) which allow to report on performance form content and export the results as needed. Hope this helps. Thank you. Nicolas

entry.title: End to End guide to setup Joule, SAP Build Process Automation, and SAP Build Work Zone on SAP BTP
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Modern enterprises rely on multiple SAP and third-party applications to run their business processes. However, navigating across different systems and extracting insights quickly can be challenging for users. To address this, SAP is embedding artificial intelligence directly into its enterprise solutions through Joule, SAP’s AI copilot.

Joule transforms how users interact with SAP systems by enabling natural language interactions, automating routine tasks, and surfacing relevant business insights instantly. Combined with SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA) and SAP Build Work Zone, organizations can build intelligent workflows, integrate APIs, and deliver AI-powered business experiences through a unified digital workspace.

1.1 What You Will Learn in This Blog

In this blog, we will walk through the end-to-end setup and integration of Joule with SAP Build services on SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP). You will learn:

  • An overview of Joule and its role as SAP’s AI copilot

  • The architecture and environment required to run Joule on SAP BTP

  • How to configure SAP Build Process Automation and create Joule skills

  • The setup steps required in SAP BTP, including Cloud Identity Services and service entitlements

  • How Joule skills interact with APIs, workflows, and destinations

  • How SAP Build Work Zone enables users to access Joule-powered experiences through a unified workspace

1.2 Joule: SAP’s AI Copilot

Joule is SAP’s AI copilot designed to simplify how users interact with enterprise applications. It provides a conversational interface that helps employees complete tasks, retrieve insights, and navigate across SAP systems more efficiently.

Designed as a companion for the Intelligent Enterprise, Joule offers role-based assistance across the SAP solution portfolio. Through natural language interactions, users can search information, access relevant applications, and perform business transactions without navigating complex menus.

By integrating directly with business applications, Joule enhances productivity, improves decision-making, and streamlines daily operations.

1.3 Environment and Architecture

Joule is built using modern cloud-native architecture on SAP BTP.

Key architectural characteristics include:

  • Runs on SAP BTP Cloud Foundry
  • Built as a multi-tenant application, ensuring strict data isolation between customers
  • Secure and enterprise-ready architecture
  • Compliance with AI ethics guidelines and GDPR standards

This architecture enables organizations to safely leverage generative AI capabilities within their SAP landscapes.

1.4 Key Features

  • Deep SAP Integration
    Joule is embedded directly within SAP applications, allowing users to interact with business processes without leaving their working environment.
  • Conversational User Interface
    The assistant provides a conversational UI built with SAP Fiori-compliant controls, enabling intuitive interactions.
  • Tailored Capabilities
    Joule adapts its assistant capabilities based on the SAP solutions implemented within the organization.
  • Continuous Innovation
    New capabilities and improvements are delivered automatically as part of SAP’s cloud updates.

2. Introduction to SAP Build Process Automation (SBPA)

SAP Build Process Automation is a low-code platform on SAP BTP that enables organizations to automate business processes, integrate systems, and build intelligent workflows.

SBPA combines multiple capabilities in one solution:

  • Workflow automation
  • Business rules management
  • AI-assisted process development
  • Joule skill creation using Joule studio

Within the Joule ecosystem, SAP Build Process Automation plays a critical role by enabling developers to create skills and workflows that Joule can trigger through natural language interactions.

For example, a Joule request such as: “Create a purchase request for office supplies” can trigger an automated workflow built in SBPA that interacts with backend systems.

2.1 Introduction to Joule Studio

Joule Studio is the development environment used to create and configure Joule skills and actions.

It allows developers to:

  • Integrate APIs
  • Define conversational capabilities
  • Build workflows
  • Connect enterprise systems
  • Test AI-assisted interactions

Joule Studio works closely with SAP Build Process Automation, enabling developers to design intelligent workflows that Joule can execute when users submit requests.

2.2 Initial Setup on SAP BTP

Before configuring Joule and related services, several prerequisites must be met in the SAP BTP cockpit.

Prerequisites

Ensure the following service plans are available in your global account:

A subaccount must be created in a Joule-supported data center region.

Step 1: Configure Custom Identity Provider (Cloud Identity Services)

Check if a Cloud Identity tenant already exists. If available, note the tenant URL: https://<tenant>.accounts400.ondemand.com. If not, create a new tenant.

Assign Entitlement

Navigate to: Subaccount → Entitlements → Add Service Plan→ Cloud Identity Services→ additional-tenant plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → Cloud Identity Services → Create Instance

Activate Tenant 

Activate the tenant through the email sent by SAP.

Step 2: Create a User in the Subaccount

Navigate to: Security → Users → Create User

Provide:

  • Name
  • Email
  • Identity Provider (Cloud Identity tenant)

Assign the required role collections to the user.

Step 3: Configure SAP Build Process Automation

Establish Trust

Subaccount → Security → Trust Configuration → Establish Trust

Enter the Cloud Identity tenant URL.

Add Entitlement

Add the service: SAP Build Process Automation – build-default plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → Process Automation → Create Instance

Assign Roles

Assign the following role collections to the Cloud Identity user:

  • Process Automation Admin
  • Process Automation Developer
  • Process Automation Participant
  • Process Automation Viewer

Step 4: Configure SAP Build Work Zone (Standard Edition)

Add Entitlement

Add the service: SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition – standard plan

Create Instance

Service Marketplace → SAP Build Work Zone → Create Instance

Assign Roles

Assign role collections such as:

  • Launchpad_Admin
  • Launchpad_User

Step 5: Run the Joule Booster

Navigate to: Global Account → Boosters

Search for: Setting Up Joule and start the booster.

Select Configuration

Choose:

  • das-application – standard plan
  • Target subaccount

Select products and integrations such as:

  • SAP Build Process Automation
  • SAP Build Work Zone

Resolving Prerequisite Errors

If prerequisites are missing, ensure the Cloud Identity tenant appears in:

BTP Cockpit → Systems

If not visible:

  • Request access to the Cloud Reporting tool: https://reporting.ondemand.com/sap/crp/cdo
  • Search for your custom tenant and copy the CIS Tenant ID. It may take a day to reflect the tenant idon cloud reporting tool after the custom tenant creation
  • Add it under: Global Account → Systems → Service Owner View→ Add Cloud Identity services system→ tenant ID

Assign Joule Roles

Create a role collection: Joule Administrator

Add the following roles from the das-application service:

  • end_user
  • extensibility_developer
  • tenant_admin

Assign the role collection to your Cloud Identity user.

Final Outcome

After completing these steps:

  • Joule is provisioned in your BTP subaccount
  • Identity trust is established
  • SBPA and Build Work Zone are integrated
  • Required roles are assigned

2.3 Joule Skill Development using SAP Build Process Automation:

SAP BTP Destinations

  • This is where you register external APIs that your Joule skill will call.
  • A destination in SAP BTP acts as a connection configuration (e.g., endpoint URL, authentication type, credentials) for external systems or APIs.
  • Example: A REST API that retrieves employee details or triggers a business workflow.

Action: You first create or register the API endpoint as a destination in SAP BTP.

Environment (in SAP Build Process Automation)

  • The environment is the runtime where Joule skills are deployed and executed.
  • It is linked to the BTP destinations you registered earlier.

Action:

  • You attach the environment to your destinations.
  • This ensures your Joule skill has permission to call those APIs at runtime.
  • The environment can also be shared with SAP Build Work Zone or Joule Standalone.

Joule Action

  • This is where you define the REST API calls your Joule skill will use.
  • You configure the GET/POST/PUT/DELETE methods, define request/response payloads, and test the API output.

Action:

  • Create a Joule Action that calls the registered destination API.
  • Validate that the API response works as expected (just like postman)

Project (Joule Skill Workflow)

  • Within SAP Build Process Automation, you create a Project that designs the skill logic (workflow) for your Joule skill.
  • You integrate your Joule Action (from the previous step) inside this workflow.
  • This project defines how the skill behaves when invoked — the steps, logic, and output.

Action:

  • Design the workflow for your skill.
  • Integrate your Joule Action using REST APIs to handle inputs/outputs.

SAP Build Work Zone
SAP Build Work Zone provides a unified digital workspace where users access business applications. It enables organizations to create business sites that bring together:

  • SAP applications (such as SAP S/4HANA)
  • Custom-built applications
  • Third-party solutions

These applications can run in the cloud or on-premises, but are delivered through a single unified user experience. Once Joule skills are created and deployed, they can be integrated into SAP Build Work Zone so users can invoke them directly from the interface.

  • This is where your application is hosted.
  • It provides the front-end interface through which users interact with Joule-integrated applications.

Action:

  • Once your Joule skill is ready, it can be integrated and accessed from within the SAP Build Work Zone.
  • This integration allows users to invoke the skill directly from the UI.

Joule Standalone

Joule can also operate independently outside of SAP Build Work Zone. In standalone mode, it still uses the same SAP Build Process Automation environment and BTP destinations, allowing Joule skills and integrations to function across different interfaces.

Shared Environment

Joule Standalone and SAP Build Work Zone share the same SAP Build Process Automation environment, which contains the configured destinations and deployed workflows. This shared setup allows developers to build and manage integrations once and reuse them across multiple interfaces, ensuring consistency and easier maintenance.

Workflow Summary:

  1. Register API: Create a destination in SAP BTP for the external API.
  2. Attach Environment: Link the environment with BTP destinations.
  3. Configure Joule Actions: Define API calls (GET/POST/PUT/DELETE) and validate responses.
  4. Build Project: Create workflow (skill logic) integrating the Joule Action.
  5. Deploy Environment: Joule deploys the skill here using attached destinations.
  6. Integrate UI: Expose the skill through SAP Build Work Zone or Joule standalone.

3. Introduction to SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone is a modern digital experience platform that enables organizations to create unified, role-based, and personalized access to applications, business processes, and information across both SAP and non-SAP systems.

It delivers a launchpad-style user experience, allowing users to access SAP Fiori applications, custom-built applications (such as CAP apps), third-party web applications, documents, and collaboration tools through a single unified interface.

By centralizing application access and content delivery, SAP Build Work Zone simplifies the user experience and improves productivity across the enterprise.

SAP Build Work Zone Editions

SAP Build Work Zone is available in two editions designed for different business needs.

Edition

Purpose

Standard Edition

A lightweight, launchpad-focused solution ideal for internal business applications and SAP Fiori experiences.

Advanced Edition

A comprehensive digital workplace solution built on SAP BTP and Cloud Identity Services, enabling intranet capabilities, collaboration features, and enterprise-wide content management.

Most application-centric implementations — including Joule integrations — typically start with the Standard Edition.

3.1 Core Components of SAP Build Work Zone

SAP Build Work Zone is composed of several key components that help organize applications, manage access, and design user experiences.

Sites

A Site represents the main digital workspace or portal where users access applications and content. It defines the overall structure and experience of the workspace, including navigation, pages, branding, and available applications.

Key characteristics:

  • Acts as the entry point for users
  • Contains pages, apps, and navigation menus
  • Supports branding and layout customization
  • Can be internal-only (Standard Edition) or public-facing (Advanced Edition)

Organizations often create different sites for departments, business units, or use cases.

Site Directory

The Site Directory is the central place where administrators manage and access all created sites.

From the Site Directory, administrators can:

  • Create new sites
  • Manage existing sites
  • Assign site administrators
  • Configure navigation and structure
  • Launch the site editor

It acts as the starting point for designing and managing digital workspaces within SAP Build Work Zone.

Content Manager

The Content Manager is the central administration tool used to manage and organize all site content within SAP Build Work Zone. It allows administrators to configure applications, pages, navigation structures, and access permissions for users.

Through the Content Manager, administrators manage several key components that define how content is presented and accessed in the workspace.

Apps

Apps represent the applications that users interact with inside SAP Build Work Zone. These applications can originate from various sources and are integrated into the workspace to provide a unified access experience.

Apps can include:

  • SAP Fiori applications
  • Custom-built applications such as CAP applications
  • External web applications
  • Embedded third-party solutions

Applications are typically integrated using:

  • SAP BTP Destinations
  • HTML5 Application Repository

This integration approach enables users to access applications seamlessly, regardless of whether they run on SAP BTP, external platforms, or on-premise systems.

Pages

Pages define the layout and structure in which applications and content are displayed within a site. Each page can contain multiple sections that organize different types of content and applications.

Common page elements include:

  • Tiles – clickable application launch icons
  • Cards – dynamic widgets that display real-time data or insights
  • Sections – logical groupings used to organize tiles and cards on a page

Pages can be created in two ways:

  • Custom-designed pages created by administrators to meet specific business needs
  • System-generated pages, such as default home pages or task-related pages like My Inbox

This flexible page structure allows organizations to design role-based and personalized digital experiences for their users.

Spaces

Spaces provide a high-level navigation structure within a site. They group related pages together and help organize the workspace for different business roles or functions.

For example, organizations might create spaces for:

  • Finance
  • Human Resources
  • Procurement
  • Analytics

Each space can contain multiple pages that organize relevant applications and content.

Catalogs

Catalogs are collections of applications that can be assigned to roles. They act as containers that group related apps together, making it easier to manage and distribute content across users.

Administrators typically organize catalogs based on business functions, departments, or solution areas.

Roles

Roles control access to catalogs and the applications within them. When roles are assigned to users, they automatically gain access to the catalogs and apps associated with those roles.

When SAP Build Work Zone is first subscribed to a subaccount, a default role called Subaccount_Everyone is automatically created. This role includes all users within the subaccount by default. Administrators can use this role to assign common content that should be accessible to every user, such as:

  • Home pages
  • General applications
  • Company-wide tools
  • Shared informational content

Additional custom roles can be created to support department-specific or role-based access control.

Channel Manager

The Channel Manager is used to import and manage business content from external providers such as SAP systems.

It allows administrators to subscribe to content channels, which automatically bring preconfigured applications into Work Zone.

Common use cases include importing:

  • SAP S/4HANA Fiori apps
  • SAP SuccessFactors apps
  • SAP Business Technology Platform content

Once a channel is subscribed, the apps become available in the Content Manager, where they can be assigned to catalogs and roles. This significantly simplifies the process of integrating SAP application content into Work Zone.

Settings and Joule Enablement

The Settings section in SAP Build Work Zone allows administrators to configure system-level features and integrations. From the settings panel, administrators can configure:

  • Site branding and theming
  • Authentication and identity integration
  • Feature toggles and extensions
  • Joule AI integration

Enabling Joule in SAP Build Work Zone

To enable Joule within Work Zone:

  1. Navigate to Site Manager
  2. Open Settings
  3. Enable the Joule AI Copilot option
     
  4. Ensure the Joule service (das-application) is correctly configured in SAP BTP
  5. Assign required Joule roles to users
  6. Once enabled, users can interact with Joule directly within the Work Zone interface, allowing them to:
  • Search for information
  • Trigger workflows
  • Access applications through natural language
  • Retrieve insights from SAP systems

This integration brings AI-powered assistance directly into the digital workspace, further improving productivity and user experience.

entry.title: Re: Incorrect iOS Icon & Splash Screen Assets
entry.content:
Hi @Tinashe_Mukombe , thank you for your post, it really helpful.
I notice that the splash screen still from AppGyver, I would like to share this piece of code to fix that:

 

The culprit is the LaunchScreen.storyboardc file located at:
/Users/m3/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/LaunchScreen.storyboardc

This compiled storyboard is showing the APPGYVER splash, ignoring the asset catalog images.

First, inspect what's inside:

ls ~/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/LaunchScreen.storyboardc/

Then look for any APPGYVER image references:

grep -r "APPGYVER\|appgyver\|launch" ~/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/LaunchScreen.storyboardc/

The fix options:

Option 1 (Easiest) - Remove the storyboard and use image-based launch:

rm -rf ~/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/LaunchScreen.storyboardc

Then remove the storyboard reference from Info.plist:

/usr/libexec/PlistBuddy -c "Delete :UILaunchStoryboardName" ~/Downloads/self-sign/ipa/Payload/RNVApp.app/Info.plist

Option 2 - Replace with your own compiled storyboard (requires an Xcode project with your splash screen compiled into a new .storyboardc file).

Start with the grep command to confirm APPGYVER is referenced inside the storyboard, then proceed with the best fix.

 

I hope this will be helpful.

entry.title: How to apply filters and select Plan Category for app Cost Center Review Booklet
entry.content:
You are using the older report apps for Cost Center data such as Cost Centre Plan/Actual.

You are now migrating to the new app Cost Center Review Booklet and want to select Plan Category and apply filters to view data in a similar manner to the older app.


------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Learn more about the SAP Support user and program here.

entry.title: Re: How to apply filters and select Plan Category for app...
entry.content:
In the report app Cost Center Review Booklet, after selecting the top filters and going to the Plan/Actual > Year-To-Date Comparison page of the new report app, there is the Side Panel section of the report app. This panel is located on the right side of the reporting section of the app.

Here, click on the Page Filters button at the top of the side panel. This will open the Plan Category selection section and Dimensions section for filtering.

To add Dimensions (Columns) for filtering, click on Select Dimensions. This will open a pop-up panel where you can select which dimensions to apply filtering, such as Cost Centers and GL Account.

entry.title: How AI and Machine Learning Are Quietly Transforming SAP Integrations — My Perspective as a Beginner
entry.content:
The Gap I Noticed in Traditional Integration
In a typical iFlow I built recently, I had to manually define rules like:
∙ If amount is greater than 100000 — route to high value channel
∙ If region is not IN or US — mark as invalid
∙ If quantity is zero — reject the item
These rules work fine when business logic is simple and stable. But what happens when the rules keep changing? What if the threshold for “high value” changes every quarter? What if new regions get added every month? A human has to go in, update the Groovy script, redeploy, test again. That is time consuming and error prone.
This is exactly where AI and ML start making sense in SAP integrations.

 

Where AI/ML Actually Fits in SAP

1. Intelligent Data Mapping

“Mail Based Batch Processing iFlow — routing logic manually defined for each invoice type” 

One of the most frustrating parts of SAP CPI for a beginner is Message Mapping. You have to manually connect every source field to every target field. For simple scenarios this is manageable. But imagine mapping two enterprise systems with hundreds of fields each.
SAP has been working on AI assisted mapping where the system suggests field mappings based on field names, data types and historical mapping patterns. Instead of manually dragging every line, the AI proposes the mapping and you just review and approve. This saves enormous time in large integration projects.

This is my data in Postman


2. Anomaly Detection in Integration Flows

“Manually setting threshold values in Content Modifier — AI could dynamically adjust these thresholds based on patterns”

Imagine your iFlow processes 10000 invoices every day. Suddenly one day 200 invoices fail. Is that normal? Is something broken? Did a source system change its format?
ML models trained on historical message processing logs can detect these anomalies automatically. Instead of waiting for someone to notice failures in the monitor, the system alerts you proactively. SAP Integration Advisor and SAP Alert Notification Service are already moving in this direction.


3. Predictive Error Handling

“Manually defined routing conditions in SAP CPI Router — AI could learn and suggest these rules automatically based on historical data”

Traditional error handling in CPI is reactive — something fails, you get an alert, you investigate. ML changes this to proactive. By analyzing patterns in past failures — which steps fail most often, at what times, with what kind of payloads — a model can predict which messages are likely to fail before they actually do and reroute them automatically.


4. Natural Language to iFlow

“Custom business logic written manually in Groovy — imagine describing this in plain English and having AI generate it automatically” 

This one genuinely surprised me when I read about it. Researchers and SAP teams are experimenting with systems where you describe what you want in plain English — something like “receive invoices from vendor system, validate amounts, send high value ones to finance team via email” — and the system automatically generates a working iFlow.
We are not fully there yet but with large language models becoming more capable this is not science fiction anymore.


5. AI Powered Document Understanding
In my Scenario 3 assignment I processed invoices that were already structured XML. But in the real world invoices come as PDFs, scanned images, emails and all kinds of unstructured formats. SAP Document Information Extraction — part of SAP AI Business Services — uses ML to read these unstructured documents and extract fields like invoice number, vendor name, amount and date automatically. This extracted data can then flow directly into an SAP CPI iFlow for further processing.

 

What SAP Is Actually Building
SAP has been investing heavily in an umbrella platform called SAP AI Core and SAP AI Launchpad. These are the infrastructure layers that allow AI models to be trained, deployed and consumed across SAP systems.
On top of that, specific AI services have been released:
∙ SAP Document Information Extraction — extracts data from unstructured documents
∙ SAP Business Entity Recognition — identifies business entities in text
∙ SAP Translation Hub — ML powered translation for global deployments
∙ SAP Intelligent Robotic Process Automation — combines RPA with ML for smarter automation
All of these can be connected to SAP Integration Suite, meaning your iFlows can call these AI services as external APIs and enrich your integration logic with intelligence.

A Small Real World Example
Let me paint a picture. Suppose a company receives invoices from 50 different vendors. Each vendor sends invoices in a slightly different format — different field names, different structures, different currencies.
Without AI:
∙ A developer manually creates one mapping per vendor
∙ 50 vendors means 50 mappings to maintain
∙ Every time a vendor changes their format someone has to update the mapping
With AI:
∙ ML model learns the pattern of each vendor’s format from historical data
∙ Automatically adapts mappings when formats change slightly
∙ Flags only major structural changes for human review
∙ Developer effort reduces from weeks to hours

 

My Honest Take


I am still a beginner with SAP CPI. I am still figuring out XPath expressions and Groovy scripts. But even at this level I can see that the manual rule based approach has limits. The more I work with integration flows the more I appreciate why AI is being brought into this space.
The integrations of tomorrow will not just move data — they will understand it, validate it intelligently, predict problems before they happen and adapt to changes automatically. SAP is clearly building towards that future and I think it is genuinely exciting for anyone working in this space today.

References and Further Reading
∙ SAP AI Business Services on SAP Discovery Center
∙ SAP Integration Advisor Documentation
∙ SAP AI Core on SAP Help Portal
∙ SAP Community blogs on Intelligent Integration

 This blog reflects my personal observations and learning while working with SAP Integration Suite as part of my coursework. All scenarios mentioned are based on hands-on assignment work.
